notes.anchor: Pompey is shut in by a siege-wall
notes.note: In March 49 BC Caesar tried to trap Pompey's army inside Brundisium by throwing moles and rafts across the harbour mouth; the report of this blockade is the disaster that prostrates Cicero here.

Minturnae was a town on the Via Appia near the mouth of the Liris, on the road between Rome and Cicero's Campanian villas; it lay on the natural route by which Caesar's despatch-riders carried news up the coast.

Atticus owned a large estate at Buthrotum in Epirus (north-western Greece) and offered it to Cicero as a refuge; Cicero calls the offer "brotherly" but it would have meant crossing to the very region Pompey was making for.

Q. Mucius Scaevola the Pontifex, the great jurist, was murdered in the temple of Vesta during the Marian terror of 82 BC; Cicero, in despair, says he now longs for 'that end of Mucius' — a noble death at home rather than flight.

The Stoic philosopher Panaetius of Rhodes was the intimate of Scipio Aemilianus, the model of the cultivated patron and his learned Greek companion; Cicero says he had honoured his own runaway tutor Dionysius even more highly, which makes the desertion sting the more.

notes.anchor: the aediles get up the games
notes.note: The aediles were junior magistrates charged with the city's public buildings, markets, and festival games; Cicero lists their humdrum routine to mark the surreal normality of Rome while Pompey is besieged.

notes.anchor: the honest men enter up their interest payments
notes.note: The "honest men" render boni, Cicero's name for the conservative propertied class; here he mocks them for placidly bookkeeping their loan interest while the Republic collapses around them.
